gold_note:
CHIEF COMPLAINT

Neck pain.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Brandon Green is a pleasant 46-year-old male who presents to the clinic today for the evaluation of neck pain. His pain began when he was involved in a motor vehicle accident in 02/2022 when he was on his way home from a pain clinic. The patient notes that he has been in 4 motor vehicle accidents; however, he notes that he was fine after the first two accidents, but the third motor vehicle accident is when his neck and back pain began. He states that he was in therapy following the second accident and had surgery after his third accident. The patient was seen at a pain clinic secondary to neck and back pain. He was prescribed fentanyl; however, he has not received a prescription for several weeks. Today, he reports that his pain is a 12 out of 10. He describes his pain as sharp and incapacitating with stiffness and pain. The patient also reports headaches, occasional dizziness. He denies any recent visual disturbances. He also reports numbness in his left arm and right leg. The patient also reports spasms throughout his body. He states that he has been experiencing fatigue since the accident. He notes that he is unable to work with this much pain.

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Constitutional: Reports fatigue.
Eyes: Denies any recent visual disturbances.
Musculoskeletal: Reports neck and back pain, and occasional swelling and bruising of the neck.
Neurological: Reports headaches, dizziness, spasms, and numbness.

PHYSICAL EXAM

SKIN: No lacerations.
MSK: Examination of the cervical spine: Pain on palpation on the bony process and muscle. Moderate ROM. No bruising or edema noted.

RESULTS

X-rays of the neck reveal no fractures.

ASSESSMENT

Neck sprain.

PLAN

After reviewing the patient's examination and radiographic findings today, I have had a lengthy discussion with the patient in regards to his current symptoms. I have explained to him that his x-rays did not reveal any signs of a fracture. I recommended an MRI for further evaluation. I have also prescribed the patient Robaxin 1500 mg every 6 to 8 hours to treat his pain. I have also advised him to utilize ice, a heating pad, IcyHot, or Biofreeze on his neck as needed. I have also provided him with a home exercise program to work on his range of motion. I advised the patient that he will not be able to work until we have the MRI results.

INSTRUCTIONS

The patient will follow up with me after his MRI for results.
notes:
Subjective:
HPI: The patient, Brandon, presents with neck pain, which started after a car crash in February of this year. He reports experiencing sharp, incapacitating pain that worsens with movement, particularly bending forward and backward. He also reports experiencing numbness in his left arm and right leg, as well as spasms throughout his body. He has had multiple car crashes in the past, including one in which he underwent surgery, but the most recent crash occurred in February of this year. He has been experiencing headaches, dizziness, and visual disturbances, but these symptoms have been relatively mild. He has been taking fentanyl for his pain, but it has not been prescribed recently. He rates his pain at a level 12 out of 10.

Past medical history: The patient has a history of car crashes, including one in which he underwent surgery, and has been in therapy for a few months after one of the crashes. He has also been to a pain clinic, where he was prescribed fentanyl.

Review of systems: The patient reports experiencing fatigue since the accident, headaches, dizziness, and visual disturbances, but these symptoms have been mild.

Current medications: Fentanyl (quantity not specified) (recently stopped)

Objective:
Vital signs: Not mentioned

Physical exam: The patient's neck pain is exacerbated by palpation on the bony process and muscle, and he is unable to move his neck from side to side. He reports pain with bending forward and backward. There is no bruising, swelling, or laceration noted.

Test Results: An x-ray was performed, which did not show a fracture.

Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has a neck sprain, which is likely causing his pain and limited range of motion.

Problem list: Neck sprain; active

Plan:
I prescribed robaxin 1500mg every 6-8 hours as needed for pain. I also instructed the patient to apply ice to the affected area when experiencing bruising and swelling, and to perform exercises to maintain range of motion. I referred the patient to physical therapy and scheduled an MRI to further evaluate the extent of the injury. I also referred the patient to pain management for consideration of local injections and further pain management options.

Follow-up: I will schedule a follow-up appointment to review the MRI results and determine the next steps in treatment.
